title: War and Peace
author: Leo Tolstoy
quotesVerified: War and Peace, Louise and Aylmer Maude translation, Project Gutenberg #2600. Quotes preserve the Maude text's accents and spellings (Borodinó, Vílna, Berëzina, Málo-Yaroslávets).
overture: Five lives and an army, across the Napoleonic wars. The searching Pierre, the disillusioned Prince Andrei, the vivid Natasha and the soldier Nikolai move through the Petersburg salons and the country estates - until in 1812 Napoleon's Grande Armée pours across the Niemen and drives to Moscow, over the top of the world the peace had built, and staggers back a ruin. The invasion drawn as the great character it is: out to Moscow at four hundred thousand, back to the river a thread.

timeline.span: 1805–1812 (the novel runs to 1820; the spine ends with the retreat)

sources: The 1812 campaign geography is historical: the Grande Armée's advance (Niemen–Vilna–Smolensk–Borodino–Moscow) and retreat (Moscow–Maloyaroslavets–Smolensk–Berezina–Vilna–Niemen) follow the documented route - the arc Charles Minard drew in his famous 1869 flow-map.

Bald Hills (the Bolkonsky estate) and Otradnoe (the Rostov estate) are fictional. Tolstoy modelled Bald Hills on his own Yasnaya Polyana near Tula, but the novel places it in Smolensk province, on the invasion road, where the French overrun it - so it is pinned there, not at Yasnaya Polyana. Otradnoe is unlocated; it is placed indicatively in the country south of Moscow.

characters.name: The Grande Armée
characters.role: Napoleon's invasion of Russia - four hundred thousand men who march east to Moscow in 1812, and the broken remnant that crawls back over the Niemen. The war drawn as a character.

characters.name: Pierre Bezúkhov
characters.role: The searching, illegitimate son suddenly made the richest man in Russia - the reader's bewildered eyes. The only principal who touches the army: on the field at Borodino, a prisoner in burning Moscow, marched with the retreat.

characters.name: Prince Andrei
characters.role: Prince Andrei Bolkónsky - the proud soldier-philosopher who chases glory to Austerlitz, finds disillusion under its lofty sky, and is mortally wounded at Borodino.

exit.note: The Borodino wound kills Andrei in the house at Yaroslávl where the Rostóvs' flight has halted, Natasha nursing him and his sister Marya arrived at the end. Tolstoy fixes no date on it beyond the autumn weeks after the battle, which is why the day carries it and the chapter cannot.

characters.name: Natásha Rostóva
characters.role: The Rostov daughter, the book's rush of life - the Otradnoe idyll, the near-ruin with Anatole, and the flight from Moscow in which she empties the family carts of treasure to carry wounded men.

characters.name: Nikolái Rostóv
characters.role: The straightforward hussar - the wars seen from the saddle. His first battles in 1805, the two Emperors embracing at Tilsit, and the rescue of Princess Marya from Bald Hills as the French close in.

locations.name: St Petersburg
locations.note: The imperial capital; the novel opens at the maid-of-honour Anna Pávlovna Schérer's soirée in July 1805, where the whole cast is introduced and war with Napoleon is the drawing-room topic.
locations.story: Where it opens: a glittering, chilly salon in July 1805, the talk all of Napoleon and Bonaparte, into which the awkward, illegitimate Pierre blunders and from which the bored Prince Andrei is escaping to the war.
locations.quote: It was in July, 1805, and the speaker was the well-known Anna Pávlovna Schérer, maid of honor and favorite of the Empress Márya Fëdorovna.

image.caption: The Palace Embankment on the Neva, 1820s - the elegant, chilly capital of the opening salons.
image.credit: Karl Beggrov, 1820s · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Moscow
locations.novelName: the Rostóvs' house, and the fire
locations.note: The old capital - the warm, noisy Rostóv household on the Povarskáya in peacetime; and, in September 1812, the city the Russians abandon and the French find already ablaze.
locations.story: The Rostóvs' Moscow: Natasha's name-day, the balls, the whole loud life of the family - and then, in 1812, the city given up to Napoleon and set alight, where Pierre stays behind and is taken prisoner.
locations.quote: bringing visitors to the Countess Rostóva’s big house on the Povarskáya, so well known to all Moscow.

image.caption: Red Square, Moscow, 1801 - St Basil's and the Kremlin wall eleven years before the fire; the living city the invasion destroys.
image.credit: Fyodor Alekseyev, 1801 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Bald Hills (Lýsye Góry), Smolensk province
locations.note: The Bolkónsky estate - fictional. Tolstoy built its severe household from his own Yasnaya Polyana near Tula, but the novel sets it in Smolensk province, squarely on the 1812 invasion road, so the pin is placed there (between Smolensk and Vyazma), not at Yasnaya Polyana, or the French overrunning it makes no sense.
locations.story: The severe country seat of old Prince Bolkónsky, where Andrei leaves his pregnant wife for the war - and which, in 1812, lies directly in the path of the advancing French: the old prince dies of a stroke as they approach, and Princess Marya is nearly trapped by her own mutinous peasants.
locations.quote: anyone who wanted to see him could come the hundred miles from Moscow to Bald Hills, while he himself needed no one and nothing.

image.caption: A faded neoclassical manor behind an overgrown garden - an indicative view of the Bolkónsky seat at Bald Hills.
image.credit: Vasily Polenov, ‘Grandmother's Garden’, 1878 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Otrádnoe (indicative, south of Moscow)
locations.note: The Rostóv country estate - fictional and, unlike Bald Hills, never tied to a named province. It is placed indicatively in the central-Russian gentry country south of Moscow, the general zone Tolstoy knew.
locations.story: The pastoral heart of the book: the wolf hunt, Christmas mummers and the moonlit sleigh-ride - and the summer night when Andrei, passing through, overhears Natasha at her window and feels life return to him.
locations.quote: In 1809 Count Ilyá Rostóv was living at Otrádnoe just as he had done in former years, that is, entertaining almost the whole province with hunts, theatricals, dinners, and music.

image.caption: A troika through the winter snow - the vitality of the Rostóvs' country idyll at Otrádnoe.
image.credit: Nikolai Sverchkov, 19th century · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Austerlitz (Slavkov u Brna), Moravia
locations.note: The 1805 battlefield in Moravia (present-day Czech Republic), Napoleon's masterpiece, where the Russo-Austrian army was broken on 2 December 1805.
locations.story: The climax of the 1805 war and Andrei's first disillusion: carrying the flag forward, he is struck down, and lies looking up at the infinite sky, all his dreams of glory suddenly small.
locations.quote: Above him there was now nothing but the sky-the lofty sky, not clear yet still immeasurably lofty, with gray clouds gliding slowly across it.

image.caption: The Battle of Austerlitz, 2 December 1805 - Napoleon and his marshals amid the captured standards and the fallen.
image.credit: François Gérard, 1810 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Tilsit (Sovetsk), on the Niemen
locations.note: The town on the Niemen where Napoleon and Alexander made peace in July 1807, meeting on a raft moored in the middle of the river.
locations.story: The peace of the two Emperors, embracing on a raft on the Niemen - which Nikolái, among the army, watches in bewilderment, unable to square the friendship of emperors with the friends he has seen die.
locations.quote: He saw the raft, decorated with monograms, saw Napoleon pass before the French Guards on the farther bank of the river, saw the pensive face of the Emperor Alexander as he sat in silence in a tavern on the bank of the Niemen awaiting Napoleon’s arrival

image.caption: The meeting of Napoleon and Alexander on the raft at Tilsit, 1807 - the monogrammed pavilion of the quote.
image.credit: Adolphe Roehn, 1808 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: The Niemen crossing, near Kovno (Kaunas)
locations.note: The Neman river near Kaunas, Lithuania, where Napoleon's Grande Armée crossed into Russia on 24 June 1812 - and, six months later, where its remnant crawled back out. The pontoon bridges were just upstream of the town.
locations.story: Where the invasion is born and where it dies. In June 1812 four hundred thousand men pour over three bridges into Russia while the Emperor watches through a spyglass; in December a frozen fraction of them recross the same river.
locations.quote: Early in the morning of the twelfth of June he came out of his tent, which was pitched that day on the steep left bank of the Niemen, and looked through a spyglass at the streams of his troops pouring out of the Vilkavisski forest and flowing over the three bridges thrown across the river.

image.caption: The Grande Armée crossing the Niemen into Russia, June 1812 - the columns pouring over the bridges, the cavalry fording the river.
image.credit: J. A. Clark, aquatint, 1816 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Vilna (Vilnius)
locations.note: Vilnius, Lithuania - the first great waypoint of the advance, where Napoleon halted; and, at the end, the town the ruined retreat staggered through in late November 1812.
locations.story: The advance's first halt and the retreat's last: Napoleon pauses here in June, master of the road to Moscow; six months later the wreck of his army drags itself back through the same streets.
locations.quote: On the twenty-ninth of November Kutúzov entered Vílna-his “dear Vílna” as he called it.
locations.imageBlank: No clean period image specific to Vílna's part in the campaign surfaced; the advance and retreat are carried by the Niemen, Smolensk, Borodino and Berëzina images. A considered blank.

locations.name: Smolensk
locations.note: The old fortress-city on the Dnieper, stormed and burned by the French in August 1812; the Russians fell back rather than give Napoleon the decisive battle he wanted. Bald Hills lies just beyond it.
locations.story: The French take Smolénsk by fire in August, and the road to Moscow - and to the Bolkónsky estate just past it - lies open. Later the starving retreat drags back through its ruins; and it is near here that Pierre, a prisoner, is at last set free.
locations.quote: Once more something whistled, but this time quite close, swooping downwards like a little bird; a flame flashed in the middle of the street, something exploded, and the street was shrouded in smoke.

image.caption: The burning of Smolénsk, August 1812 - the city ablaze across the valley, the people fleeing before the advance.
image.credit: Peter von Hess, 1846 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Borodinó
locations.note: The village ~120 km west of Moscow where the great battle was fought on 7 September 1812 - the bloodiest single day of the campaign, and the centre of gravity of the whole novel.
locations.story: The book's centre: Pierre, a civilian in a white hat, wanders the field and ends amid the carnage of the Raévski redoubt, understanding nothing and everything; Andrei's regiment stands for hours under fire, and he is mortally wounded by a shell.
locations.quote: A white church could be seen through the mist, and here and there the roofs of huts in Borodinó as well as dense masses of soldiers, or green ammunition chests and ordnance.

image.caption: Napoleon on the heights of Borodinó, 7 September 1812 - the Emperor slumped among his marshals, over a battle no one commands.
image.credit: Vasily Vereshchagin, 1897 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Voronezh
locations.note: The governorate capital on the Voronezh river, some 470 km south of Moscow and well clear of the fighting. Nikolái is sent here from the army to buy remount horses for his division, and reaches it a few days before Borodinó is fought.
locations.story: Tolstoy's own answer to what people were actually doing in the autumn of 1812: while the army fights at Borodinó and Moscow burns, Nikolái is here buying horses, going to the governor's party, and falling in love with the Princess Marya he carried out of Bald Hills and meets again by chance at her aunt's. He leaves to rejoin his regiment once the news comes; Tolstoy never says where the regiment then was, so the map leaves him at Vorónezh.
locations.quote: he learned that he was being sent to Vorónezh to buy remounts for his division, not only without regret at being prevented from taking part in the coming battle, but with the greatest pleasure
locations.imageBlank: A considered blank, the quote carrying the place. What the novel shows of Vorónezh is interiors - a hotel room, the governor's party, Princess Marya's face across a church - and the one period view to surface, Vasily Timm's lithograph of the town from the west, is a generation after 1812 and of a skyline the book never looks at. Vílna and Yaroslávl are blank for the same kind of reason.

locations.name: Yaroslávl
locations.note: The city north-east of Moscow where the fleeing Rostóvs halt, and where the wounded Prince Andrei - carried unknowing among them out of Moscow - dies with Natasha nursing him.
locations.story: Where the flight from Moscow comes to rest and the deathbed is set: Natasha, and then Marya, nurse the dying Andrei through his last days, until he reaches a strange serene acceptance and is gone.
locations.quote: When they reached Yaroslávl the wound had begun to fester (Natásha knew all about such things as festering) and the doctor had said that the festering might take a normal course.
locations.imageBlank: No clean public-domain period view of Yaroslávl fitting the deathbed beat surfaced; a considered blank, the quote carrying the place.

locations.name: Málo-Yaroslávets
locations.note: The town south-west of Moscow where, in October 1812, the battle forced Napoleon off his hoped-for southern retreat and back onto the same ravaged road he had come by - the turn where the army's disintegration began.
locations.story: The hinge of the retreat: barred from a fresh road south, the French are forced back onto their own devastated track, and the long starving, freezing death-march begins in earnest.
locations.quote: Dokhtúrov again whom they sent to Formínsk and from there to Málo-Yaroslávets, the place where the last battle with the French was fought and where the obvious disintegration of the French army began

image.caption: The Battle of Málo-Yaroslávets, October 1812 - the town that turned the retreat back onto its own ravaged road.
image.credit: Peter von Hess, mid-19th century · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: The Berëzina crossing, near Borisov
locations.note: The river near Barysaw (Borisov), Belarus, where the retreat met its catastrophe in late November 1812; the actual crossing was on improvised bridges at Studzianka, a few kilometres upstream of the town.
locations.story: The catastrophe of the retreat: at the broken bridges over the half-frozen river the whole misery of the army is concentrated into one moment, and thousands drown or are cut down in the crush.
locations.quote: at the broken bridge across that river the calamities their army had been previously enduring were suddenly concentrated at one moment into a tragic spectacle that remained in every memory

image.caption: The retreat: Napoleon and his marshals in the frozen birch woods - the ruined army crawling west toward the Berëzina.
image.credit: Vasily Vereshchagin, ‘On the High Road’, 1887–1895 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

movements.routeSource: The Grande Armée's line of advance, June 1812: from the Niemen crossing to Vilna.
movements.note: The invasion pours in and drives on Vilna.

movements.routeSource: The advance from Vilna east to Smolensk, July–August 1812.
movements.note: East into the heartland, toward Smolensk.

movements.routeSource: Smolensk to the field of Borodino, late August–September 1812.
movements.note: On toward Moscow, to the great battle at Borodino.

movements.routeSource: Borodino to Moscow, entered 14 September 1812.
movements.note: The last march to Moscow - the goal, at four hundred thousand dwindled.

movements.routeSource: The retreat from Moscow in late October 1812, turned back at Maloyaroslavets.
movements.note: The retreat begins - south, until Maloyaroslavets bars the way.

movements.routeSource: Forced back onto the ravaged Smolensk road, late October–November 1812.
movements.note: Back onto the ruined road it came by, starving and freezing.

movements.routeSource: Smolensk to the Berezina crossing at Studzianka, late November 1812.
movements.note: On to the Berezina, and the catastrophe.

movements.routeSource: The wreck of the army from the Berezina back through Vilna, early December 1812.
movements.note: A thread now, dragging back through Vilna.

movements.routeSource: The remnant recrosses the Niemen, December 1812.
movements.note: Back over the Niemen - of four hundred thousand, a fraction. The war-character dies where it was born.

movements.routeSource: Pierre's move from Petersburg society to Moscow after his sudden inheritance; illustrative line.
movements.note: The new-made heir, from the capital to Moscow.

movements.routeSource: Pierre rides out from Moscow to the army on the eve of Borodino; illustrative line.
movements.note: The civilian rides out to see the battle.

movements.routeSource: Pierre returns to Moscow after Borodino and stays for the fire; illustrative line.
movements.note: Back to Moscow, to stay behind for the fire.

movements.routeSource: Pierre is marched west with the prisoner column of the retreating army and freed by partisans near Smolensk; illustrative line along the retreat road.
movements.note: Marched as a prisoner with the dying army, and freed near Smolensk.

movements.routeSource: Prince Andrei joins the 1805 campaign in Austria; illustrative line to the field of Austerlitz.
movements.note: To the war, chasing his Napoleon-worship and glory.

movements.routeSource: Andrei returns home wounded to Bald Hills, where his wife dies in childbirth; illustrative line.
movements.note: Home wounded, to grief - his wife dies as he arrives.

movements.routeSource: Andrei passes Otradnoe on estate business in 1809 - the night he overhears Natasha; illustrative line.
movements.note: Passing Otradnoe - and hearing Natasha at her window.

movements.routeSource: Andrei rejoins the army for 1812 and commands a regiment at Borodino; illustrative line.
movements.note: Back to the war, to command a regiment at Borodino.

movements.routeSource: The mortally wounded Andrei is carried from Borodino through Moscow and out with the Rostóv convoy to Yaroslavl; illustrative line via Moscow.
movements.note: Carried wounded from the field, through Moscow, to his deathbed.

movements.routeSource: The Rostóvs to their country estate for the 1809 idyll; illustrative line.
movements.note: The Rostóvs to the country - the hunt, the moonlit night.

movements.routeSource: Back to Moscow for the ball season and the near-ruinous affair with Anatole Kuragin; illustrative line.
movements.note: Back to Moscow, and the near-ruin with Anatole.

movements.routeSource: The Rostóvs' flight from burning Moscow, the carts given to the wounded, to Yaroslavl; illustrative line.
movements.note: The flight from Moscow - the carts emptied of treasure to carry the wounded.

movements.routeSource: Nikolái rides to his first campaign, 1805, ending in the rout at Austerlitz; illustrative line.
movements.note: To his first war, and the rout.

movements.routeSource: Nikolái on service to the 1807 peace at Tilsit; illustrative line.
movements.note: On service, to the peace of the Emperors at Tilsit.

movements.routeSource: Nikolái, foraging with his squadron in 1812, reaches Bald Hills in time to rescue Princess Marya from her mutinous peasants; illustrative line.
movements.note: To Bald Hills - in time to carry Princess Marya out of the invasion's path.

via.name: Kaluga
via.note: The army's rear base in 1812 and the road hub of the whole southern quarter - the way out of the Smolensk country for anyone not going to Moscow.

via.name: Tula
via.note: Where the road down from Kaluga meets the Moscow-Vorónezh tract.

via.name: Yelets
via.note: A staging town on the tract, in the black-earth country.

movements.routeSource: No road is named in the text - Tolstoy gives the destination and 'post horses', nothing more (Bk 12, Ch. 4), and post horses meant the post road. The documented way into Vorónezh from the north is the Moscow-Vorónezh tract through Tula, Yefremov, Yelets and Zadonsk, one of the seven ancient state roads, laid out under Peter I in 1700. Reconstructed rather than documented because Nikolái sets out from the army in Smolensk province, not from Moscow: the link south through Kaluga to pick the tract up at Tula is assembled, not sourced.
movements.note: South to buy remounts for the division - out of the war, in the week the army marches to Borodinó.

story.title: Anna Pávlovna's soirée
story.narration: St Petersburg, July 1805: a glittering salon where the talk is all of Napoleon and the coming war. Into it blunders the awkward, illegitimate Pierre Bezúkhov; out of it slips his one friend, the proud and weary Prince Andrei, bound for the army.

story.title: Pierre inherits
story.narration: Pierre's dissolute father dies, and the awkward bastard is overnight the richest man in Russia - swept from Petersburg to Moscow and into marriage with the beautiful, cold Hélène, the first of the wrong turns his searching life will take.

story.title: The Rostóvs' name-day
story.narration: In Moscow, the warm and noisy Rostóv household keeps Natasha's name-day - the girl a rush of life against the Petersburg chill - while her brother Nikolái, giddy with it, prepares to ride off and be a hussar.
That autumn the young men went to their first war - the campaign of 1805, fought far from Russia on Austrian soil.

story.title: Nikolái rides to war
story.narration: Nikolái rides west with the cavalry to Austria, to learn in one autumn what a battlefield actually is.
And to that same field, by his own road, Prince Andrei.

story.title: Andrei seeks his glory
story.narration: And Andrei goes to the same war on Kutúzov's staff, hungry for a moment of Napoleonic glory of his own.

story.title: The lofty sky
story.narration: At Austerlitz, on 2 December 1805, Napoleon breaks the Russian and Austrian armies. Andrei, seizing a fallen standard and running forward, is struck down - and lies looking up at the infinite grey sky, every dream of glory suddenly small and false.

story.title: Home to grief
story.narration: He is carried home wounded to Bald Hills, the severe estate of his father - and arrives just as his young wife dies giving birth to their son.
Meanwhile Nikolái soldiered on through the years between the wars.

story.title: To the peace of Emperors
story.narration: Nikolái, on service through the years that follow, comes in 1807 to Tilsit on the Niemen, where the war is to be ended by the two Emperors themselves.

story.title: The raft on the Niemen
story.narration: He watches Napoleon and Alexander embrace on a raft in the middle of the river, all monograms and ceremony - and cannot make it fit with the friends he has watched die. The same Niemen will be crossed in earnest five years on.
And back in Russia, through the long peace that followed, life simply went on.

story.title: To the country
story.narration: The long peace: in 1809 the Rostóvs go down to Otrádnoe, the pastoral heart of the book - the wolf hunt, the Christmas mummers, the moonlit sleigh-ride.

story.title: The moonlit night
story.narration: Here Natasha is most herself - and on a summer night, at her window, so full of life she can hardly bear it, she is overheard by a passing traveller who thought his own life over.
And that same summer Andrei was on the roads near Otrádnoe.

story.title: Andrei overhears her
story.narration: That traveller is Andrei. Passing Otrádnoe on estate business, he hears the girl at the window above him - and, against all his resolutions, feels the spring come back into his blood.

story.title: Back to Moscow
story.narration: Betrothed to Andrei but made to wait a year, Natasha is carried back into the Moscow season - where the worthless, beautiful Anatole Kuragin nearly ruins her, and the engagement is broken.
And then, in June 1812, the peace the book has built is simply overrun. On the far bank of the Niemen, Napoleon puts a spyglass to his eye.

story.title: The crossing
story.narration: Some four hundred thousand men pour over three bridges into Russia - the largest army the world has seen; a Polish regiment drowns trying to swim the river to impress the Emperor. From here, the width of the line is their number - watch it thin.

story.title: The advance
story.narration: The great blue line - four hundred thousand strong - drives east and takes Vilna, where Napoleon halts, master already of the road into the heartland.

story.title: On to Smolensk
story.narration: On east through the summer heat, the Russians always falling back, refusing battle. Sickness, desertion and the corps peeled off to the flanks tell terribly: barely a hundred and seventy-five thousand reach Smolénsk, and storm and burn it in August.
And Bald Hills lies just beyond Smolénsk, directly in the army's path - where the invasion reaches a family's own door.

story.title: The rescue of Marya
story.narration: Foraging with his squadron, Nikolái reaches Bald Hills to find the old prince dead of a stroke, the French almost arrived, and Andrei's devout sister, Princess Marya, trapped by her own mutinous peasants - and carries her out to safety, and toward the rest of both their lives.

story.title: Toward Moscow
story.narration: A hundred and forty-five thousand drive on toward Moscow, and the Russians turn at last to give battle on the field of Borodinó - the bloodiest single day of the whole war.
And one of ours was going the other way entirely - south, out of the war, on the army's errands.

story.title: Buying horses at Vorónezh
story.narration: Nikolái is not at Borodinó. Days before the battle he is sent south with post horses to buy remounts for his division - and passes the worst weeks of the war in a quiet governorate town, at the governor's party, meeting again the Marya he carried out of Bald Hills.
To that field, from their different lives, three of our people are drawn at once.

story.title: Andrei's regiment
story.narration: Andrei, long back in the army, brings his regiment up to stand in reserve on the field.

story.title: Pierre rides out
story.narration: And Pierre, a civilian in a white hat and top hat, rides out from Moscow simply to see the great battle for himself.

story.title: Borodinó
story.narration: On 7 September 1812, the bloodiest day of the war. Pierre wanders onto the Raévski redoubt amid the smoke and the falling men, handing up ammunition, understanding nothing and everything - that no one commands this, that it simply devours.

story.title: Andrei's wound
story.narration: Andrei's regiment stands for hours under artillery without firing a shot, men dropping around him. A shell lands at his feet; he will not throw himself down; and it takes him - the mortal wound that carries him, slowly, out of the war and out of the world.

story.title: Into Moscow
story.narration: Borodino settles nothing, but the road lies open. The hundred thousand who are left - a quarter of those who crossed the Niemen - march into Moscow, to find it deserted, and, that night, on fire.
Pierre too turned back from the field toward Moscow.

story.title: Pierre stays behind
story.narration: Pierre goes back into Moscow and, half-mad, stays behind as everyone flees - on a secret plan to kill Napoleon himself.

story.title: The fire, and captivity
story.narration: Moscow burns on every side. Pierre rescues a child from the flames, is seized as an incendiary, nearly shot - and is held prisoner, where a peasant, Platón Karatáev, quietly teaches him how to live.
And the Rostóvs, fleeing the doomed city, carry out of it - without knowing it - the dying man among the wounded.

story.title: The flight from Moscow
story.narration: Natasha has the family's loaded carts emptied of their treasure to carry wounded soldiers instead - and so the Rostóv convoy pulls north for Yaroslávl, bearing among the wounded a man in a fever she does not at first recognise.
And the wounded man was carried along that same northward road.

story.title: Carried to his death
story.narration: For the wounded man is Andrei, carried from Borodinó through Moscow and out with the convoy - brought, by chance, to the very people he loved.

story.title: The deathbed
story.narration: At Yaroslávl, Natasha nurses him through his last days, and his sister Marya comes; and Andrei, past bitterness now, reaches a strange wide peace, and dies - the war's cost, come home.

story.title: The retreat begins
story.narration: Winter closing in, Moscow a trap, Napoleon marches his hundred thousand out - tries for a fresh road south, and is turned at Málo-Yaroslávets back onto the same ravaged track he came by.
And Pierre is dragged along that road with the prisoner column - the invasion's captive, walking its death-march.

story.title: The prisoner's march
story.narration: West through the freezing ruin Pierre marches, watching the gentle Karatáev shot when he can walk no further - until partisans fall on the column near Smolénsk and set the prisoners free. The Rostóvs' eager, boyish youngest, Pétya, is killed in the raid.
And the army itself dragged on westward, dying as it went.

story.title: Starving and freezing
story.narration: Now the ash-grey thread of the retreat. Past Smolénsk fewer than sixty thousand are left, starving and freezing and dissolving - no longer an army, only men trying to get home.

story.title: The Berëzina
story.narration: Thirty-odd thousand reach the half-frozen Berëzina, and the whole misery gathers into one moment: the improvised bridges collapse under the crush, and thousands drown or are cut down at the water's edge.

story.title: Back through Vilna
story.narration: Some twelve thousand stagger on through Vilna, barely an army at all now - a thread of frozen men where a flood had passed.

story.title: Back over the Niemen
story.narration: And back at last over the Niemen it crossed six months before: ten thousand crawling out of four hundred thousand. The war-character dies at the river where it was born; and behind it, changed and alive, Pierre walks free into the rest of his life.
